---
title: "Script 03 — Oaxaca-Blinder Decomposition"
subtitle: "TFM: Gender Wage Gap in Spain | MESIO UPC-UB | Julia Anglada Lomaeva | 2025-26"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    theme: flatly
    highlight: tango
    code_folding: show
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE,
                      fig.width = 9, fig.height = 5.5, dpi = 150)
```

```{r main}
# =============================================================================
# Prerequisites: run 01_preprocessing.R and 02_OLS_models.R first.
# This script assumes df_main and spec2_formula are in the environment.
#
# What this script does:
#   1. Runs the Oaxaca-Blinder decomposition using Spec 2 controls
#   2. Uses the Neumark (1988) pooled reference — avoids the index number problem
#   3. Breaks down the endowment and coefficient components by variable group
#   4. Produces the three-panel decomposition figure (Figure 4.2 in TFM)
#   5. Interprets results: is it a "what you have" or "what you get" problem?
#
# References:
#   Oaxaca (1973)              — original decomposition (male reference)
#   Blinder (1973)             — independent parallel development
#   Neumark (1988)             — pooled reference coefficients
#   Cotton (1988)              — weighted decomposition alternative
#   Fortin et al. (2011)       — comprehensive review of decomposition methods
#   De la Rica et al. (2008)   — OB applied to Spanish wage gap
# =============================================================================

library(tidyverse)
library(oaxaca)     # oaxaca() function — the standard R implementation
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)

if (!exists("df_main")) stop("Run 01_preprocessing.R first.")
if (!exists("spec2_formula")) stop("Run 01_preprocessing.R first.")
cat("Oaxaca-Blinder script loaded. n =", nrow(df_main), "\n\n")
```

```{r auto-load, include=FALSE}
# Auto-load preprocessed data if not in environment
if (!exists("df_main")) {
  message("Running preprocessing...")
  source("00_preprocessing.R", local = FALSE)
}
```


```{r}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 1: THE OB DECOMPOSITION FORMULA
# =============================================================================
# The oaxaca() function requires a specific formula syntax:
#   outcome ~ controls | group_variable
#
# We use spec2_formula controls (the extended specification with MERCADO and
# REGULACION) for consistency with the OLS extended model. This is important:
# the OB decomposition decomposes EXACTLY the same gap as ols_spec2 — using
# a different set of controls would make the results incomparable.
#
# The formula object created in 01_preprocessing.R uses as.formula(), which
# oaxaca() cannot directly parse. We rebuild it here as a string formula.

#equivalent to spec2_formula
ob_rhs <- paste(
  c("education", "tenure", "I(tenure^2)", "age_group",
    "occupation", "sector", "contract_type", "working_day",
    "supervisory_role", "region", "firm_size", "firm_sector",
    "market_scope", "collective_bargaining"),
  collapse = " + "
)

ob_formula <- as.formula(
  paste("log_hourly_wage ~", ob_rhs, "| female")
)

df_ob <- df_main %>%
  mutate(
    female                = as.integer(gender == "Female"),
    education             = as.integer(education),
    age_group             = as.integer(age_group),
    occupation            = as.integer(occupation),
    sector                = as.integer(sector),
    contract_type         = as.integer(contract_type),
    working_day           = as.integer(working_day),
    supervisory_role      = as.integer(supervisory_role),
    region                = as.integer(region),
    firm_size             = as.integer(firm_size),
    firm_sector           = as.integer(firm_sector),
    market_scope          = as.integer(market_scope),
    collective_bargaining = as.integer(collective_bargaining)
  )

ob_result <- oaxaca(
  formula       = log_hourly_wage ~ education + tenure + I(tenure^2) +
    age_group + occupation + sector + contract_type +
    working_day + supervisory_role + region + firm_size +
    firm_sector + market_scope + collective_bargaining | female,
  data          = df_ob,
  R             = 500,
  group.weights = NULL
)

cat("Oaxaca-Blinder formula (female = 1 for women, 0 for men):\n")
```

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 2: FIT THE DECOMPOSITION
# =============================================================================
# group.weights: controls which wage structure is used as the counterfactual.
#
#   group.weights = 1   → male coefficients as reference (Oaxaca 1973)
#   group.weights = 0   → female coefficients as reference (Blinder 1973)
#   group.weights = NULL → Neumark (1988) pooled reference (our choice)
#
# Why Neumark (NULL)?
# The "index number problem" (Oaxaca 1973, Reimers 1983): the endowment
# and coefficient components change depending on which group's coefficients
# are used as the counterfactual wage structure. Using male coefficients
# overstates the endowment effect; using female coefficients understates it.
# Neumark's solution: use the pooled regression coefficients (from a model
# on the full sample with a gender indicator) as the counterfactual.
# This is the most defensible and widely cited choice.
# FIX: pass group.weights = NULL for Neumark pooled reference.
#
# R = 500: bootstrap replications for SEs on decomposition components.
# ~3-5 minutes on this sample size.

cat("Fitting Oaxaca-Blinder decomposition (Neumark pooled reference)...\n")
cat("(Bootstrap R = 500 — this takes ~3-5 minutes)\n\n")

set.seed(2024)
ob_result <- oaxaca(
  formula      = ob_formula,
  data         = df_ob,          # FIX: use df_ob with female = 0/1
  R            = 500,
  group.weights= NULL            # FIX: NULL = Neumark (1988) pooled reference
)


cat("Decomposition complete.\n\n")
```

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 3: EXTRACT AND INTERPRET RESULTS
# =============================================================================

# ── 3.1 Three-fold decomposition (overall) ───────────────────────────────────
# The three-fold breakdown separates the gap into:
#   Endowments:    due to compositional differences (what workers have)
#   Coefficients:  due to wage structure differences (what the market pays)
#   Interaction:   cross-term (would exist even with identical endowments
#                  and identical returns — pure covariance term)
#
# For reporting, we collapse into two-fold by absorbing the interaction into
# the coefficient component (following Fortin et al. 2011). This is the
# most common reporting convention in applied economics.

three_fold <- ob_result$threefold$overall
cat("=== THREE-FOLD DECOMPOSITION ===\n")
print(three_fold)

# Extract scalars
gap_total   <- three_fold["coef(total)"]
gap_end   <- three_fold["coef(endowments)"]
gap_coef  <- three_fold["coef(coefficients)"]
gap_inter <- three_fold["coef(interaction)"]

# SE from bootstrap
se_end  <- three_fold["se(endowments)"]
se_coef <- three_fold["se(coefficients)"]

# Two-fold (absorbing interaction into coefficient component)
gap_total    <- gap_end + gap_coef + gap_inter
gap_unexplained <- gap_coef + gap_inter
gap_explained   <- gap_end

cat(sprintf("\n=== TWO-FOLD SUMMARY ===\n"))
cat(sprintf("Total gap (log points):               %+.4f  (%.2f%%)\n",
            gap_total, (exp(gap_total)-1)*100))
cat(sprintf("  Endowment (explained):              %+.4f  | %.1f%% of total\n",
            gap_explained, gap_explained/gap_total*100))
cat(sprintf("  Coefficient + interaction (unexpl): %+.4f  | %.1f%% of total\n",
            gap_unexplained, gap_unexplained/gap_total*100))

# In % wage terms (exp(coefficient) - 1 approximation)
cat(sprintf("\nIn percentage wage terms:\n"))
cat(sprintf("  Total gap:             %.2f%%\n", (exp(gap_total) - 1) * 100))
cat(sprintf("  Explained component:   %.2f%%\n", (exp(gap_explained) - 1) * 100))
cat(sprintf("  Unexplained component: %.2f%%\n", (exp(gap_unexplained) - 1) * 100))


# ── 3.2 Variable-level contribution to ENDOWMENT component ───────────────────
# Which variables drive the compositional differences between male and female
# workers? This tells us WHERE the human capital differences are concentrated.
# It's a numeric matrix — convert directly using column names

vars_df <- as.data.frame(ob_result$threefold$variables) %>%
  tibble::rownames_to_column("variable") %>%
  rename(
    coef_end   = `coef(endowments)`,
    se_end_var = `se(endowments)`,
    coef_coef  = `coef(coefficients)`,
    se_coef_var= `se(coefficients)`,
    coef_inter = `coef(interaction)`,
    se_inter   = `se(interaction)`
  )

# Endowment component by variable
end_by_var <- vars_df %>%
  filter(variable != "(Intercept)") %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(coef_end))) %>%
  mutate(pct_of_endowment = round(coef_end / gap_end * 100, 1))

cat("=== ENDOWMENT COMPONENT — by variable ===\n")
print(end_by_var %>% select(variable, coef_end, se_end_var, pct_of_endowment))


# ── 3.3 Variable-level contribution to COEFFICIENT component ─────────────────
# Which variables drive the structural differences in how the market rewards
# male vs. female workers? This is where discrimination manifests.

# Coefficient component by variable
coef_by_var <- vars_df %>%
  filter(variable != "(Intercept)") %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(coef_coef))) %>%
  mutate(pct_of_coef = round(coef_coef / gap_coef * 100, 1))

cat("\n=== COEFFICIENT COMPONENT — by variable ===\n")
print(coef_by_var %>% select(variable, coef_coef, se_coef_var, pct_of_coef))
```

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 4: THE KEY DIAGNOSTIC QUESTION
# =============================================================================
# Is the Spanish gender pay gap a "what you have" problem (endowments)
# or a "what you get" problem (coefficients)?
#
# Literature benchmark: Blau & Kahn (2017) find that for the US, the
# coefficient component accounts for roughly 50-60% of the gap.
# De la Rica et al. (2008) find that for Spain, the pattern differs
# between the bottom and top of the distribution.
# Our finding with 2022 data tests whether this has changed.

pct_unexplained <- gap_unexplained / gap_total * 100

cat(sprintf("\n=== DIAGNOSTIC INTERPRETATION ===\n"))
if (pct_unexplained > 60) {
  cat(sprintf("%.1f%% of the gap is unexplained by observable characteristics.\n",
              pct_unexplained))
  cat("This is a 'what you get' problem: the labour market structurally\n")
  cat("rewards equivalent endowments differently by gender.\n")
  cat("Policy implication: pay equity audits, promotion criteria reform.\n")
} else if (pct_unexplained > 40) {
  cat(sprintf("%.1f%% of the gap is unexplained — a mixed picture.\n",
              pct_unexplained))
  cat("Both compositional differences and structural penalties contribute.\n")
} else {
  cat(sprintf("%.1f%% of the gap is unexplained.\n", pct_unexplained))
  cat("The gap is primarily compositional — endowment differences dominate.\n")
  cat("Policy implication: focus on horizontal segregation (sector/occupation).\n")
}
```

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 5: VISUALISATIONS
# =============================================================================

# ── Figure 4.2a: Waterfall chart of two-fold decomposition ───────────────────
waterfall_data <- tibble(
  Component = c("Total Gap", "Explained\n(Endowments)",
                "Unexplained\n(Coefficients + Interaction)"),
  Value     = c(gap_total, gap_explained, gap_unexplained),
  Fill      = c("total", "explained", "unexplained")
)

fig_waterfall <- ggplot(waterfall_data,
                        aes(x = Component, y = (exp(Value) - 1) * 100, fill = Fill)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "#555555") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "total"       = "#1A1A2E",
    "explained"   = "#2E75B6",
    "unexplained" = "#C00000"
  ), guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title    = "Oaxaca-Blinder Decomposition (Two-Fold)",
    subtitle = "Neumark (1988) pooled reference | EES 2022",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "% wage gap contribution"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

# ── Figure 4.2b: Top drivers of the ENDOWMENT component ─────────────────────
fig_endowment <- end_by_var %>%
  slice_head(n = 10) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(variable, coef_end),      # ← coef → coef_end
             y = (exp(coef_end) - 1) * 100,         # ← coef → coef_end
             fill = coef_end > 0)) + 
  geom_col(width = 0.65, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "#555555") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "#2E75B6", "FALSE" = "#9DC3E6"),
                    guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title    = "Endowment Component by Variable",
    subtitle = "Positive = men have more; negative = women have more",
    x        = NULL, y = "% wage contribution"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10)

# ── Figure 4.2c: Top drivers of the COEFFICIENT component ────────────────────
fig_coefficient <- coef_by_var %>%
  slice_head(n = 10) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(variable, coef_coef),      # ← coef → coef_coef
             y = (exp(coef_coef) - 1) * 100,         # ← coef → coef_coef
             fill = coef_coef > 0)) +  
  geom_col(width = 0.65, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "#555555") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "#C00000", "FALSE" = "#E8B4B4"),
                    guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title    = "Coefficient Component by Variable",
    subtitle = "Negative = market undervalues female workers on this dimension",
    x        = NULL, y = "% wage contribution"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10)

fig_ob_full <- fig_waterfall / (fig_endowment | fig_coefficient)
ggsave("fig_oaxaca_blinder_full.png", fig_ob_full,
       width = 12, height = 10, dpi = 200)
print(fig_ob_full)
cat("\nSaved: fig_oaxaca_blinder_full.png\n")
```

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 6: ROBUSTNESS — ALTERNATIVE REFERENCE GROUPS
# =============================================================================
# We report the Neumark decomposition as the main result.
# As a robustness check, we also compute with male and female references
# to show the range of the index number problem on this dataset.
# If the unexplained component is large across all three references,
# the finding is robust to this choice.

cat("\nFitting alternative reference decompositions for robustness...\n")

ob_male_ref <- oaxaca(ob_formula, data = df_ob, R = 200,
                      group.weights = 1)   # male coefficients as reference
ob_fem_ref  <- oaxaca(ob_formula, data = df_ob, R = 200,
                      group.weights = 0)   # female coefficients as reference

# Helper function to extract unexplained component from any ob object
get_unexplained <- function(ob_obj) {
  ov <- ob_obj$threefold$overall
  as.numeric(ov["coef(coefficients)"]) + as.numeric(ov["coef(interaction)"])
}

robustness_refs <- tibble(
  Reference   = c("Neumark (pooled)", "Male reference", "Female reference"),
  Unexplained = c(
    get_unexplained(ob_result),
    get_unexplained(ob_male_ref),
    get_unexplained(ob_fem_ref)
  )
) %>%
  mutate(
    Pct_of_Total = round(Unexplained / gap_total * 100, 1),
    Pct_penalty  = round((exp(Unexplained) - 1) * 100, 2)
  )

cat("\n=== INDEX NUMBER ROBUSTNESS CHECK ===\n")
print(robustness_refs)
cat("(Stable % unexplained across references = robust finding)\n")
```

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 7: SAVE OUTPUTS
# =============================================================================

write_csv(end_by_var,       "results_ob_endowment_by_var.csv")
write_csv(coef_by_var,      "results_ob_coefficient_by_var.csv")
write_csv(robustness_refs,  "results_ob_reference_robustness.csv")

# Save the full OB result object for later use
saveRDS(ob_result, "ob_result.rds")

cat("\n=== SCRIPT 03 COMPLETE ===\n")
cat("Key result:\n")
cat(sprintf("  Total gap:     %.4f log points (%.2f%%)\n",
            gap_total, (exp(gap_total)-1)*100))
cat(sprintf("  Unexplained:   %.1f%% of total\n", pct_unexplained))
cat("\nNext: source('04_DML.R')\n")
```
